comments.title: Anger as a result of magic
comments.content: Whenever I try to practice spirituality, I find myself feeling a lot of anger, about a very wide range of things. I want to be spiritual but find the anger that comes up when I pursue it too disruptive of my life. What options are there for handling this?

replies.title: Re: Anger as a result of magic
replies.content:
Nearly always when that happens, you've got a lot of anger that you haven't dealt with, and it's bottled up below the surface; the moment you go into yourself, there it is. What makes this complex is that anger, according to esoteric psychology, is usually a secondary emotion; what that means is that there's some other emotion that's more difficult to bear -- fear, grief, and shame are the most common -- as the primary emotion, and you've taught yourself to feel angry instead because that's less painful than feeling the primary emotion.

The best way I know of to deal with this is journaling. Get a notebook and a pen, and for fifteen minutes each day, have a written conversation with your anger. Ask it questions, and write down whatever it says in response, no matter how uncomfortable that makes you; don't censor anything. Keep this practice up day after day, letting your anger find its voice in the harmless context of words in a notebook. Eventually you'll start getting glimpses of the primary emotion under the anger, and then you can dialogue with that and work through it. It's not a fast process, but many people find that this is an effective way to get in "under the hood" of your emotional life and let the stuff you've been bottling up rise to consciousness and release the energy locked up in it.

replies.title: Re: Anger as a result of magic
replies.content: How long does this usually take? I've tried journaling, but after a couple months it's still not helping much. I know more about what I'm angry over (instead of general anger), but it's still there, and I'm not sure what it's from.

replies.title: Re: Anger as a result of magic
replies.content: It depends entirely on how much anger you've got bottled up and how central it is to the internal architecture of your psyche. It may take quite a while.

replies.title: Re: Anger as a result of magic
replies.content: If I can throw in my two cents, I would suggest that it may also be helpful to learn about Mars (which rules anger among other things) in your natal chart and watch Mars transits to your natal chart. Your two month time frame for journaling marks the period when Mars came out from under the shadow of its June-Aug retrograde (it came out from under the shadow Oct 8). If I'm understanding your comment correctly, and you did start journaling about two months ago, the retrograde in Aquarius might have brought something up. Also, I like to fight fire with fire, and frequently recommend to clients that they engage in structured Martial activities (such as competitive sports or martial arts) to take the edge off while they work on the deeper understanding and source of the anger. When you're starting to get a grip on the anger through journaling and these other methods, learning to switch that energy towards its positive expression (passion, ambition, physical vigor, and motivation are my favorites) can also help direct its influence in a productive manner. Finally, sometimes it can be beneficial to check with a doctor. I have had clients, particularly females but could be either, who found that their hormones were out of balance. Good luck with your work! I've worked through a lot of Mars issues myself. In my experience, Mars settled down once I was truly doing the work I was meant to be doing (after twenty years of study, practice, and development) -- Joy Vernon

replies.title: Re: Anger as a result of magic
replies.content:
I read about this thing called Emotional Scale which runs from Depression to Joy, like this:

1 . Joy/Appreciation/Empowered/Freedom/Love
2 . Passion
3 . Enthusiasm/Eagerness/Happiness
4 . Positive Expectation/Belief
5 . Optimism
6 . Hopefulness
7 . Contentment
8 . Boredom
9 . Pessimism
10. Frustration/Irritation/Impatience
11. Overwhelment
12. Disappointment
13. Doubt
14. Worry
15. Blame
16. Discouragement
17. Anger
18. Revenge
19. Hatred/Rage
20. Jealousy
21. Insecurity/Guilt/Unworthiness
22. Fear/Grief/Depression/Despair/Powerlessness

So, the drill is to go up the scale one notch at a time.
Lets say you feel “depressed”. To improve your emotion would not be to jump up to nr. 1 at once, in fact, it would be very hard to do that, so what you do is you think thoughts that creates feelings of perhaps “guilt” within you. Stay there for a while until you really feel it – then climb higher up.
Maybe now you can create feelings of “rage” or "anger"...Then "blame"...and so on
Every emotion is an improvement and you will always want to go upwards towards "joy, freedom".

Yeah I know, easier said than done.
Anyway, as a last resort remember George Harrison:
"Still it only takes time 'til love comes to everyone"

comments.content: How does one determine whether two systems of magic are compatible or not? I feel very drawn to the system of magic you've outlined in the DMH, but I've also been reading several books on witchcraft (not specifically Wicca, though they have a fair bit of Wiccan influence) and feel drawn to that as well. The magic described in the witchcraft books I've read is primarily natural magic; while there is a ritual component as well, it doesn't seem as intensive as anything described your books. Do you think these systems are likely to be safe to combine, or should I pick one to focus on and leave the other alone for now?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In my experience, you should learn one and only one system of magic at a time. That doesn't mean you should only learn one system of magic -- I've learned upwards of a dozen -- but the best way to do it, and in many cases the only safe way to do it, is to take up one course of study, follow it from beginning to end, and then start on another. So I'd encourage you to pick one for now, and leave the other for a couple of years from now, when you've got the first one down pat.

replies.title: more than one system at a time
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender)

JMG, the advice you are giving on this seems to me to be a good general rule, but I think it may apply mainly to full fledged systems that go from basic principles and step by step acquisition of skills to more advanced work. Most of the introductory books and websites on witchcraft are not teaching a magical system. What they offer may be magical, but it is not systematic.

Typically they combine some natural magic with non-ecstatic devotional practices and a few exercises in grounding, centering and visualization. Some people find the devotional side of witchcraft to be very satisfying and do not have an interest in any magic other than natural magic, nor do they have an interest in cultivating unfamiliar states of consciousness.

Witchcraft of this sort seems to me to be pretty compatible with other kinds of European magic. I can see it possibly getting in the way if one is tasked with learning an elaborate and specific set of magical correspondences to structure one's psyche, and the associations one is already using for devotional practices and natural magic are different. I don't think it would be harmful, but it would make learning the full-fledged magical system a little harder because of the need to set aside previous mental associations.

People who take up witchcraft and want to do something more intense than what I just described are few. If they are loners and self directed, they usually do extensive research in various sorts of magic and experiment on their own until they have constructed an idiosyncratic system that they practice by themselves. Others, if they have the opportunity, find a teacher or a coven in hopes of getting some additional training in group magical ritual and controlled, intentional ASCs--which they may or may not get, depending on the teacher or coven. When people join a coven, if it's a hierarchical coven, the coven leaders will let them know what else is okay for them to be engaged in during their training and what to let alone. If it's a stone-soup sort of coven, everybody is going to be studying something different and sharing it with the group, and eventually if the coven lasts, it will create some kind of syncretic coven tradition out of those contributions.

replies.title: Re: more than one system at a time
replies.content: So noted, but I still think the principle stands. If a witchcraft tradition doesn't actually have that much to teach, fine, but it seems to me that you need to be able to commit fully to any magical or spiritual tradition while learning it, and not try to hop back and forth between systems with significantly different ways of thinking and working.

comments.content:
Jumping off from the discussion of emerald engagement rings, I’m curious about green emeralds vs white (goshenite) or white vs blue vs other colors of sapphires in the same role. White emeralds and sapphires are sometimes marketed as more honest budget alternatives to diamond vs cubic zirconium. Are diamonds safe if they are the wearer’s birthstone, as discussion indicated might be true for opal?

Also, you mention a dearth of mages able to work out the natural magical properties of newer materials like platinum, cubic zirconium, titanium, tungsten, etc. Would discursive meditation be a valid mode of inquiry or would the results be too specialized, gnosis of individual intricacies relating to the material but lacking universal applicability?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) All of these are excellent questions to which, as far as I know, the traditional lore offers no answers. Your second question is therefore highly relevant.

2) Discursive meditation is a beginning, but it's far from sufficient in itself. To work out the properties of a material substance when these are unknown, it's necessary to gather any folklore, traditional or modern, that may have gathered around the substance; to use discursive meditation to explore the material and its folklore, then to use some means of directly contacting spiritual realities, such as scrying or evocation, to get more information. Then, once a hypothetical set of correspondences and properties has been worked out, those have to be put to the test of magical practice -- for example, does a ring made of platinum effectively resonate with (say) the planetary energies of Uranus, and does it (say) have the property of helping the wearer preserve his or her independence of mind and will? That's something that has to be tested repeatedly before such indications can be treated as standard.

comments.content: What are the most powerful forms of magic cleansing and protection, and under what circumstances would you use them?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Nobody knows them any more. Remember that magic is still in the early stages of being recovered after the dark age that began with the Scientific Revolution; we've lost a fantastic amount of knowledge -- and what mages had during the Renaissance, the last great era of magic, was only a fragment of what was known in ancient times.

Fortunately, enough good workable methods of magical cleansing and protection are known that pretty much every tradition of magic has at least one. Thus your best bet is not to worry about which is "best" in some abstract sense, but learn whatever working is provided in the tradition you're studying, and get really, really good at it through relentless practice.

replies.title: Re: Magic and belief
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender)

My primary witchcraft tradition, NROOGD, did public rituals six to eight times a year for several decades, starting in 1969. We got more than a warm fuzzy feeling on a number of occasions, though certainly not at every sabbat. I credit a number of factors: a) We cast a good tight circle. b) During NROOGD's heyday, spectators outside the circle were outnumbered by participants about thirty to one. c) More than half the participants were initiates of the tradition, who knew the basic ritual by heart and had worked extensively with it in their covens. d) The rituals included a number of light trance-inducing elements, so that any people who participated, regardless of their beliefs or lack thereof, tended to get a direct experience of at least some of the shifts in energy. e) by doing the same basic ritual both privately and publicly dozens and dozens of times, the tradition developed an egregore.

What NROOGD did was exceptional. It is very hard to keep a group going that pours a lot of time and energy into both regular public ritual and private initiatory work without burning out. You need a lot of people, good direction and favorable circumstances.

replies.title: Re: Magic and belief
replies.content: A fine example of an exception that proves the rule. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Mars = Diamond Correspondence
replies.content:
Do you remember what texts you were translating?

I started reading Kunz and on p. 4 he gives diamond to the Sun. I then checked the index -- he has a lot of entries for that stone. The chapter dedicated to it mentions it as "sun" and "king" to the pearl's "moon" and "queen." Brings victory (martial). Kunz cites Rueus ("De gemmis"), who "calls it 'a gem of reconciliation,' as it enhanced the love of a husband for his wife." Other references, before we head off into different cultural associations, might suggest rulership by Saturn or Jupiter. Sex and reproductive powers are also attributed to the diamond (relevant to use in wedding rings). "The spirit dwelling in the stone was thought to take offense at being bought and sold, and was supposed to depart from the stone..." unless offered as a pledge of love.

I checked Picatrix (your translation) and Book 2 Ch 10 has diamond under both Saturn and the Sun. Agrippa gives diamond to Mars (but Agrippa strikes me as spastic, he gives emerald to three planets). High Magic by Levi (your translation) gives diamond to Mars, and basically makes the other associations you mention in the comments to the original post.

My boyfriend Hal researched the following: In the bible, the word diamond comes from the Hebrew HLM meaning "to strike down, to hammer, stamp, conquer, disband, beat down, break down, overcome, smite with the hammer" (Strong's Lexicon)--all very martial. It's not definitive that the word refers to the diamond, though. Diamond on the breastplate is the tribe of Zebulon, which Mathers assigns to Capricorn (bringing us to Saturn). Godwin concurs. There are many alternate associations.

I can see any of these associations. Diamond can be like Saturn because it is transformed under intense pressure and time. It can be like Mars because of its hardness and ability to cut. It is like the Sun in that it has light and clarity. Each of these makes sense. The folklore supports all of them. How do I determine a system to positively assign this stone to a particular rulership?

I want there to be a continuous thread of essential meanings that can be traced through time. Not meanings that bounce from planet to planet as we progress from author to author. Any guidance in pinning down floating correspondences would be appreciated.

Ok, you mentioned working with the stone. I do have a ring with a small diamond chip set in gold; it was my high school class ring. Of course, I had not started doing any magic work at this time. I liked the ring, but never liked the school I went to. (I now respect it as having provided a solid, basic, foundational education.) I probably wore the ring for about a year. It would fall off my finger at times, at other times it would feel tight. After leaving for college, I never lived in the town of my high school again for more than about three months. I still have the ring though. I suppose I would associate those experiences more with Mars than any of the other planets. But could be Saturn. Also, I was born in April, Aries, and diamond is my birth stone, for what that's worth.

However, reading the comments to the OP, there seemed to be many people who strongly valued both their commitment and their diamond ring. That would swing the whole meaning over to Saturn. Saturn will bring to an end anything that isn't lasting. And when Saturn's rules are satisfied, he brings great commitment (sometimes stuckness). So when considering the idea of an engagement/wedding ring, I think the Saturn association works the best--it can bring about the endings/divorce, but when the fit is right, it honors lasting commitment. Under a Saturn rulership, using the diamond for sealing a relationship would require that the relationship is based on respect and commitment.

Thoughts?

-- Joy Vernon

replies.title: Re: Mars = Diamond Correspondence
replies.content: Simply that this is pretty standard in natural magic -- it's far from rare to find conflicting correspondences in different sources. You may want a coherent thread of meaning, but unfortunately that's not what the surviving texts provide! So your task is the same one that mages have been wrestling with for the last couple of millennia: sorting through the fragmentary remnants of the magical systems of the past and trying to piece together something that works.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Question about enchantment:

For the past few years I've noticed this interesting phenomenon concerning many people, especially those who watch television and identify with "the system". There is a curious tendency for men to play at being jollily incompetent genial dullards with barely concealed rage and women to play at being compassionately overwhelmed shrewish nags with barely concealed rage. Over and over again going about the world I see these same gendered caricatures worn like masks, enough that it turns me into a recluse!

What I find so odd about this is that these sorts of behaviors consist of exactly what get you turned into an animal in old school fairy tales. What I mean is that these are clearly unattractive and destructive ways of being in the world, and yet they form basic gendered archetypes for, at least, the well-to-do. I sincerely wonder, what gives? Why do people act like such cartoonish versions of unbearable people? What's your take on this JMG?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Those are old entrenched media patterns, older even than the rise of TV. (But compare the dynamics between the principal characters in "I Love Lucy.") Now that TV is doing mroe to raise chidren than many sets of parents, it stands to reason that the pattern is becoming more common. Also. rage (and any anger) is hugely addictive, maybe even more so than anxiety.

One of the things Elva and I noticed right after we got married and had our oldest child (1967 and 1968), was how much the women's magazines in the supermarkets played with these two things, and suggested as the only possible relief that their readers should devote more and more of their energy to consumer behavior.

For example, nearly every issue would have at least two articles clearly designed to produce major anxiety in a woman reader about her physical attactiveness to her husband (and her weight issues), and at least three articles offering ways to cook rich meals and deserts in the interest of satisfying their husbands in ways that don't require a woman's being physically attractive--usually meals that required high-end ingredients from the supermarket. So ... your anxiety leads you to shop for such meals and cook them, which doesn't help with your weight problem, which makes you even more anxious, which starts the whole vicious cycle over again. And you get angry about being trapped in the vicious cycle. Pretty soon anxiety and anger cause the cycle to become a highly addictive pattern of weekly behavior, which greatly increases the profits of both supermarkets and publishers of women's magazines.

This sort of toxic marketing seems to be everywhere now. Ugh! That was what really got me to stop watching TV back in the '80s. I am old enough to remember when we were first of all citizens, not consumers, and I watched the change from the first to the second happen. It made me a passionate anti-consumer ... though a somewhat inconsistent one, as I still buy far too many books for our groaning bookshelves.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The sorcerers of the advertising agencies and the mass media (which is a wholly owned subsidiary of the advertising agencies, remember) have been casting that particular curse on people for many years now. It's a very effective bit of nasty magic, as it alienates people from each other and from themselves, so they can be more easily goaded into thinking they can fill the void by buying things.

Good for you for catching the fairy tale analogy! Of course you're quite right, and in fact they have been turned into animals -- that peculiarly degraded species of animal known as the consumer. Consulting a bestiary, I find that consumers are related to swine, though most swine insist angrily that this isn't the case; consumers, after all, are far greedier and muddier than swine, and also noticeably less intelligent. The most notable characteristic of consumers is that hunters can trick them very easilyinto stampeding over a cliff by telling them that something off in that direction will make them feel good. Physiologus, of course, uses this as a metaphor for why it's a bad idea to believe what you hear from the media. ;-)

replies.title: Re: Banishing versus invoking pentagram pattern
replies.content:
1) If you mean the specific way of drawing the pentagrams I've included in the rituals posted here, the only source I know right now is in my book The Celtic Golden Dawn. I'll see if I can find an image and post it here sometime soon.

2) Start with your birth chart. Find the signs on the cusp of your first, second, fifth, and tenth houses; find the planets ruling each of these signs; and choose the planet that has the best astrological dignity -- that means (a) in the sign of its rulership or exaltation; (b) in the first, fourth, seventh, or tenth houses; (c) receiving a trine or sextile from at least one other planet, and not receiving a square or opposition from any planet; or (d) better still, more than one of these.

Now look at the ephemeris for December, and find a date when the Moon is applying to a trine or sextile with the position where the planet you've chosen is in your natal chart. (Not where it currently is in the sky. If your natal Jupiter, let's say, is in the 14th degree of Pisces, find the Moon making trine or sextile to that degree, not to wherever Jupiter happens to be at the moment.) Make sure no other planet is applying to a square or opposition with the planet you've chosen on that day. That's your day for the release.

comments.title: Discursive Meditation, Prayer, & Choosing a Pantheon
comments.content:
**2nd post. Forgot to sign in the first time. :) **

Hi JMG,

As always, love Magic Monday.

I practiced discursive meditation for the first time last week, and to my surprise, I dig it.

I've practiced popular "mind clearing" meditation for a number of years, and my habitual thinking showed much resistance to your descriptions of discursive meditation. The idea of actively thinking during mediation seemed the antithesis of meditative practice.

Thankfully, your writing won me over. Powerful practice in a short time.

A few quick, related questions...
1. Who/what are we contacting with discursive meditation? Our Higher Self?
2. Discursive meditation feels similar to prayer to me. IE - Asking a question and seeing where the "dialogue" takes you. How does it differ from prayer?
3. If I have not chosen a Pantheon yet, who am I praying to right now? Higher Self?
4. Should I make choosing a Pantheon a high priority to make prayer more effective?

Much thanks.

replies.title: Re: Discursive Meditation, Prayer, & Choosing a Pantheon
replies.content:
1) It depends. You can certainly get to the point where that's what you're contacting; in the early stages, you're usually working with what Jung called unconscious contents more or less constellated around an archetype, and what older traditions might call spirits.

2) With prayer you're reaching for something definitely outside of, and greater than, yourself. It's not just a dialogue, it's a dialogue with a god or goddess.

3) That's like asking "who am I talking to?" Well, who are you facing toward, and whose name are you calling?

4) Not necessarily. You can also let the pantheon choose you, you know.

replies.title: Re: Discursive Meditation, Prayer, & Choosing a Pantheon
replies.content:
I was not aware I could let the Pantheon choose me.

Any steps I would need to take?

Any benefits/drawbacks to playing the passive role in that selection?

replies.title: Re: Discursive Meditation, Prayer, & Choosing a Pantheon
replies.content:
Pray for guidance and pay attention to answers, which may come in the form of coincidences or sudden intuitions.

The great advantage of letting a pantheon choose you is that you know the deities actually want to work with you. They aren't appliances; they have their own intentions and desires, and the mere fact that you want to work with a pantheon does not guarantee that the pantheon wants to work with you.

replies.title: Re: Allergies
replies.content:
I'm not sure if you will get this message but I'll chime in with my experience in case it can help you.

I used to have terrible hayfever, as well as cat and other allergies. They completely disappeared when I began eating raw sauerkraut and paying attention to my gut bacteria. I cut out processed foods, sugars and grains, which were feeding the bad gut bacteria, and instead helped good gut bacteria to grow.

If I stop eating sauerkraut the allergies come back after a period of time. When I start again, it takes a few days but the allergies disappear again.

replies.title: Re: Allergies
replies.content:
I used to be very sensitive to tobacco smoke and allergic (sneezing from being near, itching where touching) horses and to a lesser degree most dogs. I have used homeopathic remedies as prescribed by a trained practitioner for over thirty years. My bedroom is downwind from the horse barn, petting horses and all kinds of dogs doesn't bother me. I still don't like the smell of cigarettes or cigars (pipes and natural tobacco products have less effect) but I don't stuff up or get watery eyes. I also have not had an asthma attack in ever so long--don't even bother keeping an inhaler around anymore. I actually sought treatment mainly for chronic depression, which seems mostly under control--unless you count a concern and sadness over the messed up state of the world as depression. For many people the major problem with homeopathic treatment is the requirement to give up coffee--no decaf, no coffee-period. Also no camphor products or tea tree oil or electric blankets. Those are less of a problem for most. As always--your mileage may vary. The second problem is that relief is not overnight. It may take months of treatment with different remedies to peel away symptoms. Cost is also a factor as a practitioner may spend 60-90 minutes on an intake interview and must charge for their time--so an appointment may run $2-300 or more. I'm not up on current pricing since I have been with the same practitioner for so long. But OTH, the remedies themselves are usually in the $20-40 range and that is for a treatment that may last months or years--a far cry from the hundreds of dollars a month of many allopathic drugs. For mild symptoms first aid level remedies available at health food stores may suffice--get a good book first so you're not working just with the package label. The National Center for Homeopathy website lists several. They and the American Institute of Homeopathy also have directories of practitioners. Anyone with small children in the home might want to have Arnica Montana 5x or 30C handy for falls, bumps and bruises, also the 'worked too hard first day in garden or gym' aches.

Hope this helps,

Rita

comments.title: Combust planets and grand trines
comments.content:
Greetings. A couple astrology questions.

I was born on the "Harmonic Resonance", a day of world wide meditation because the planets had some interesting alignments. Does this day have any special significance? Or was this a case of a lot of hype? What is your interpretation of grand trines?

How do you interrupt combust planets, conjunction with the sun? Does this mean they are over-powered by the sun to the point of being indistinct? How would you interpret a cluster of planets in conjunction with the sun? This doesn't seem to be cover in A-Z Horoscope Maker.

Thanks for this forum.

replies.title: Re: Combust planets and grand trines
replies.content:
1) As far as I can tell, the "Harmonic Resonance," like the "Harmonic Convergence" that preceded it, were pure hype. Grand trines are just trines cranked up in volume; they tend to make your personality easygoing and bring you a certain degree of easy success in the fields ruled by the planets involved.

2) In a natal chart, the effects of combustion aren't really significant. When a planet is conjunct the sun, that means that whatever the planet rules plays a central role in your personality (since the Sun rules the personality). I've got Mercury conjunct Sun in my natal chart, for example, and what that means is that I basically can't separate my personality from my mentality -- my thoughts, my inner life, my communicative acts are central to who I am, or at least to who I perceive myself to be.

replies.title: Harmonic Convergence
replies.content:
The publicity about the Harmonic Convergence resulted in a great many people all over the world --hundreds of thousands at least-- meditating on peace and love on the same day at noon local time. I was one of those people. I didn't believe in the importance of the planetary positions, but I figured, take a few minutes of my lunch hour meditating on peace, couldn't hurt.

About six months later, Gorbachev and the rulers of East Germany allowed the populace to demolish the Berlin Wall and removed the physical and legal obstacles to travel out of the Warsaw Pact countries to Western Europe. I had not expected that to happen in my lifetime. Post hoc ergo propter hoc is a logical fallacy, but these two events were without historical precedent and I thought and still think they may have been connected.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I haven't read any of the recent works on "R'lyehian spirituality" or the like; Kenneth Grant always struck me as a put-on artist of the Andy Warhol variety. The versions of the Great Old Ones presented by Lovecraft and most of his fellow Mythos authors are unbalanced in certain typical ways that, to my mind, make those forms of the deities in question unsafe to work with -- though of course your mileage may vary.

When I started working out the inverted Lovecraftian pantheon for The Weird of Hali, I reworked the deities extensively to make them fit classic patterns in occult philosophy and polytheist spirituality. replies.title: Re: Ninth Planet?
replies.content: If they actually discover a planet of any size (and not just a bunch of minor planetlets like Pluto and Eris), then it'll be time to try to figure out what new influence it represents. Myself, my guess is that Neptune's the furthest astrologically effective planet, and the snowball worldlets of the Van Oort cloud will turn out to be no more significant than the asteroids, if that -- but we'll see.

replies.title: Re: Ninth Planet?
replies.content: I know it's a little late, but if I may ask, what made Pluto a temporary planet?

replies.title: Re: Ninth Planet?
replies.content: According to astrological theory, planets aren't discovered by accident, and they aren't dropped from the list of planets by accident, either. When Pluto was first discovered, astronomers thought it was as large as the Earth; later research downgraded it in size and importance until, in 2006, the body of astronomers tasked with such things ruled that Pluto wasn't a planet at all, just a minor body like an asteroid. The interesting thing is that between 1930 and 2006, it functioned astrologically as a planet, and there was a period of thirty years or so (one Saturn cycle) before 1930 when its influence was increasingly evident; thus I expect it to have a similar "tapering-off" period from 2006 to 2036 or so.

comments.title: "Circle of the place"
comments.content:
Cheers and happy Monday, JMG-

I am practicing very early stages of the GD system, and I've noticed a phrase that occurs several times in connection with several of the rituals, and which I had mostly been discounting since it doesn't seem to be mentioned outside Regardie's big book of GD papers- even though, in retrospect, it sounds important. That phrase is "complete the circle of the place," such as in the description of the LBRP, where it's noted "at all times complete the circle of the place before commencing an invocation." I feel like I'm probably missing something blindingly obvious, but I don't see an explanation of the phrase; what does it mean?

My guess is that it means circumambulation, in which case I have a second question; I have limited private space at present, so my ritual space is just barely enough that I can stand in one place and not hit a wall with an outstretched arm. To perform the LBRP I simply turn in place. How can I best circumambulate in such circumstances?

Many thanks!

-A

replies.title: Re: "Circle of the place"
replies.content: Nah, it's much simpler than that. When you do the LBRP, you trace a pentagram in the east, then draw a line around to the south; trace a pentagram in the south, then draw a line around to the west; trace a pentagram in the west, and then draw a line around to the north; trace a pentagram in the north, and then complete the circle of the place by drawing the line around to the east. The point of the phrase is that you make the line go all the way around -- you don't stop in the north and leave a gap to the northeast. That's all.
